verdict_reasoning: C3 is complement component 3, synthesized predominantly by hepatocytes and secreted into plasma as a soluble protein. Upon activation, C3 is cleaved to C3a (soluble anaphylatoxin) and C3b, which becomes covalently attached via its thioester to nearby surfaces including pathogen surfaces and host cell surfaces/ECM — but this is matrix/target deposition, not anchoring to a dedicated partner TM protein on the producing cell. Therapeutic antibodies (e.g., pegcetacoplan targets C3) engage the soluble circulating pool. Ruling out contextual buckets: not cell-state-induced surface display; not tissue-restricted surface; not lysosomal exocytosis; no documented PM pool with own anchor (dual_localization n/a); covalent deposition onto bystander surfaces is not wash-resistant attachment to a defined TM partner of C3.
predicted_key_uncertainty: C3b thioester-mediated covalent tagging of cell surfaces could arguably be framed as stable_surface_attachment, but it lacks a dedicated TM partner.
